region_of_origin: China (Lanzhou, Gansu Province); served in Singapore
food_subgroup: Noodle soup with beef tendon and pickled vegetables

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for golden soup pickled cabbage beef tendon noodles. FOODON_00002572 (noodle dish) is the closest defensible parent category.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A00LC (Noodles, cooked) is the closest parent. No specific code exists for this composite dish with beef tendon and pickled cabbage in a golden broth.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept found for this specific composite noodle dish.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard restaurant bowl of golden soup pickled cabbage beef tendon noodles, approximately 550 g, comprising noodles, braised beef tendon, pickled cabbage, and golden broth.

nutrient_source.title: Beef noodle soup, composite dish estimation based on USDA FoodData Central component data: cooked wheat noodles (FDC ID 168936), braised beef tendon (FDC ID 174032 beef shank/tendon analogue), and sauerkraut/pickled cabbage (FDC ID 169279)
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service, FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact entry for golden soup pickled cabbage beef tendon noodles exists in HPB Singapore Foodo or USDA FoodData Central. Values are estimated by weighted component analysis: approximately 45% cooked wheat noodles, 25% braised beef tendon, 15% pickled cabbage, and 15% golden broth (turmeric-based). Per-100g values are derived from this composite. Energy cross-checked against comparable Chinese beef noodle soup entries in USDA SR Legacy and FAO/INFOODS SEA reference data.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of wheat noodles and analogous noodle soup dishes; reference: Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values: 2008. Diabetes Care. 2008;31(12):2281-2283.
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database; peer-reviewed journal (Diabetes Care)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for golden soup pickled cabbage beef tendon noodles. GI of 47 is estimated based on the GI of wheat noodles (GI ~47, low GI category) as reported in the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care). The pickled cabbage (suan cai) and protein-rich beef tendon components are expected to further attenuate postprandial glucose response. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (47 × 74.25) / 100 ≈ 34.9, where carbohydrate per 550g serving = 13.5 × 5.5 = 74.25g.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content from pickled cabbage (suan cai) and seasoned broth
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit consumption of this dish due to its estimated sodium content of approximately 480 mg per 100g (approximately 2,640 mg per bowl), which substantially exceeds the recommended single-meal sodium target of 600–800 mg; requesting reduced-sodium broth and limiting the soup liquid consumed can help mitigate risk.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate glycemic load from wheat noodles, partially offset by protein and pickled vegetable content
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should be aware that a standard bowl provides an estimated glycemic load of approximately 35, driven by the wheat noodle component; choosing a smaller portion, requesting extra beef tendon and vegetables in place of noodles, and pairing with a non-starchy side can help moderate postprandial blood glucose response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Elevated sodium and moderate saturated fat from braised beef tendon and broth
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should note that this dish contributes significant sodium (approximately 2,640 mg per bowl) and moderate saturated fat from the beef tendon and broth; limiting portion size, avoiding the full broth, and choosing this dish infrequently within a heart-healthy dietary pattern is advisable.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Golden Soup Pickled Cabbage Tender Beef Tendon Noodles (金汤酸菜嫩牛筋面, jīn tāng suān cài nèn niú jīn miàn) is a Chinese noodle soup dish originating from the Lanzhou beef noodle tradition of Gansu Province, China, and widely served in Chinese restaurants across Singapore. The dish features hand-pulled or machine-cut wheat noodles served in a golden-coloured broth — typically achieved through the use of turmeric, egg yolk, or a combination of spices — and topped with slow-braised beef tendon that has been cooked until gelatinous and tender. Fermented pickled cabbage (suan cai, 酸菜) provides a sharp, tangy counterpoint to the rich broth, while chilli oil, spring onion, garlic, and sesame oil contribute aromatic depth. The texture profile is complex: the noodles are chewy and springy, the beef tendon is soft and yielding with a collagen-rich mouthfeel, and the pickled cabbage adds crunch and acidity. Nutritionally, the dish is a moderate-energy meal providing approximately 112 kcal per 100g, with meaningful protein from the beef tendon (approximately 7.8g per 100g) and carbohydrate from the noodles (approximately 13.5g per 100g). The primary clinical concern is sodium: the pickled cabbage and seasoned broth contribute an estimated 480 mg sodium per 100g, translating to approximately 2,640 mg per standard bowl, which is a significant consideration for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The glycemic index is estimated at 47 (low GI), based on the wheat noodle component, with the protein and fermented vegetable content expected to attenuate postprandial glucose response. This dish is not suitable for vegetarians or vegans, contains gluten and soy allergens, and is not certified halal due to the beef tendon preparation method varying by restaurant.

confidence_reason: No exact entry for this specific dish was found in HPB Singapore Foodo, ICMR-NIN, or USDA FoodData Central. Nutrient values are estimated by weighted component analysis of cooked wheat noodles, braised beef tendon, pickled cabbage, and golden broth using USDA FoodData Central component data. GI is estimated from the closest analogous food (wheat noodles, GI 47) from the University of Sydney GI Database. Confidence is medium rather than low because the component data sources are high-quality approved sources and the estimation methodology is transparent and defensible.
notes: Nutrient values are estimated from component analysis using USDA FoodData Central data for cooked wheat noodles (FDC ID 168936), beef tendon/shank analogue (FDC ID 174032), and pickled cabbage/sauerkraut (FDC ID 169279), weighted at approximately 45%, 25%, 15%, and 15% broth respectively. Vitamin D is -1 as it is not reported in the component source data and is not expected to be a significant contributor in this dish. Vitamin E is -1 as it is not reported in the component source data for this dish type. Selenium is -1 as it is not reported in the component source data. Trans fat is 0.0; confirmed as negligible/trace in the component foods per USDA FoodData Central. Sodium estimate of 480 mg per 100g reflects the pickled cabbage and seasoned broth contribution and should be treated as an approximation; actual restaurant sodium may vary significantly. Halal status is not confirmed as it depends on individual restaurant sourcing and preparation. The dish is served at Lan Ting Xu Lanzhou Beef Noodles (兰亭序) at Lazada One, Singapore, at SGD 16.90. GI of 47 is an analogous estimate based on wheat noodles; no direct GI measurement for this composite dish was found in approved sources.
